does: This systematization of knowledge paper provides a comprehensive taxonomy of prompt hacking attacks against large language models, distinguishing and analyzing three primary threat categories: jailbreaking (bypassing safety constraints), prompt injection (inserting malicious instructions into inputs), and information leaking (extracting sensitive model or training data). The authors develop a novel evaluation framework that moves beyond binary response classification to a five-class system, enabling more fine-grained assessment of LLM behavior across different failure modes. The work synthesizes existing attack methodologies, clarifies definitional boundaries between overlapping attack types, and establishes structured approaches for understanding how adversarial prompts compromise system safety and reliability.
connects: This work builds on growing research into LLM vulnerabilities and adversarial attacks, extending earlier work on alignment failures and instruction-following robustness. It relates to prior investigations of jailbreaking techniques that emerged as these models became more capable, efforts to extract training data through membership inference and model inversion, and prompt injection research emerging from widespread deployment of LLM APIs. The systematization connects to broader AI safety literature addressing robustness evaluation, but distinguishes prompt-specific attack vectors from general adversarial examples. The framework contribution responds to limitations of binary success/failure metrics in prior security evaluations, drawing on more nuanced assessment methodologies from cybersecurity and software security domains.
enables: This systematization provides foundational structure for future defensive research, enabling development of targeted mitigations for each attack category with clearer threat models and evaluation criteria. The five-class response framework allows more sophisticated benchmarking of prompt defense mechanisms and safety training techniques, supporting iterative improvement cycles. The work facilitates standardized evaluation protocols for LLM safety, helping practitioners and researchers compare defense strategies rigorously. It points toward more principled approaches to building robust LLM systems through better understanding of failure modes, potentially informing designs of constitutional AI methods, robust prompting techniques, and architectural defenses that address specific vulnerability patterns identified in the taxonomy.
